item.title: Prompt is All You Need: Prompting Foundation Models for Large-scale Self-supervised Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Jiaojiao Su
Qiwu Luo
Shuzhou Sun
Yuenan Hou
Xinyu Zhang
Janne Heikkilä
Chunhua Yang
Li Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: This paper addresses the important and challenging task of large-scale unsupervised semantic segmentation (LUSS). We present the first attempt to unleash the power of foundation models (FMs) for the challenging, dense prediction task LUSS, and our main objective is to present simple, effective yet efficient solutions for LUSS, namely Prompting foundation models for LUSS (PLUSS). Firstly, we proposed a cascade framework PLUSS _ α \_\alpha by effectively marrying CLIPS, Grounding DINO, and SAM in a zero-shot manner. This cascade architecture automatically generates semantic and spatial prompts for SAM, establishing a strong baseline that significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods. Building upon this foundation, we propose PLUSS _ β \_\beta , which addresses the critical bottleneck of prompt quality through two novel tuner modules: a semantic tuner that enhances fine-grained category discrimination via visual prompt tuning, and a box tuner that improves object localization through cross-modal feature fusion. Both tuners are optimized by capitalizing on the knowledge already present within the foundation models themselves, deriving self-supervised signals from internal model consistency. This approach requires no external supervision or updates to the foundation models' parameters. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S benchmarks demonstrate that PLUSS _ β \_\beta achieves remarkable performance improvements, surpassing the previous best method by 39.6%, 27.3%, and 22.6% in mIoU for 50, 300, and 919 categories respectively. Our approach exhibits robust category-shape representation across varying object sizes and dataset scales, while maintaining strong generalization capabilities for open-vocabulary tasks. The proposed framework provides a solid baseline for adapting foundation models to downstream vision tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/Miss-Jo/PLUSS_beta. Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:59+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.858 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiaojiao Su; Qiwu Luo; Shuzhou Sun; Yuenan Hou; Xinyu Zhang; Janne Heikkilä; Chunhua Yang; Li Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3673339

Relevance: 0.858 (must_read)

This paper addresses the important and challenging task of large-scale unsupervised semantic segmentation (LUSS). We present the first attempt to unleash the power of foundation models (FMs) for the challenging, dense prediction task LUSS, and our main objective is to present simple, effective yet efficient solutions for LUSS, namely Prompting foundation models for LUSS (PLUSS). Firstly, we proposed a cascade framework PLUSS _ α \_\alpha by effectively marrying CLIPS, Grounding DINO, and SAM in a zero-shot manner. This cascade architecture automatically generates semantic and spatial prompts for SAM, establishing a strong baseline that significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods. Building upon this foundation, we propose PLUSS _ β \_\beta , which addresses the critical bottleneck of prompt quality through two novel tuner modules: a semantic tuner that enhances fine-grained category discrimination via visual prompt tuning, and a box tuner that improves object localization through cross-modal feature fusion. Both tuners are optimized by capitalizing on the knowledge already present within the foundation models themselves, deriving self-supervised signals from internal model consistency. This approach requires no external supervision or updates to the foundation models' parameters. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S benchmarks demonstrate that PLUSS _ β \_\beta achieves remarkable performance improvements, surpassing the previous best method by 39.6%, 27.3%, and 22.6% in mIoU for 50, 300, and 919 categories respectively. Our approach exhibits robust category-shape representation across varying object sizes and dataset scales, while maintaining strong generalization capabilities for open-vocabulary tasks. The proposed framework provides a solid baseline for adapting foundation models to downstream vision tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/Miss-Jo/PLUSS_beta.

item.title: Active Style-Content Dual-Branch Domain Adaptation for Semi-Supervised SAR Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Xi Yang
Quantao Xie
Yirong Yang
Nannan Wang
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images offer unique advantages in all-weather, all-day remote sensing, but the high acquisition costs and time-consuming annotation processes limit their widespread implementation. Semi-supervised domain adaptation leverages abundant annotated optical images and a small number of labeled SAR images to achieve great performance on SAR images. However, existing semi-supervised domain adaptation object detection methods typically select SAR domain labeled samples randomly, making it difficult to fully exploit the valuable information and distinctive features inherent in the target domain data. Moreover, there is a significant style and content gap between optical and SAR images, and previous methods have not adapted to them in a task-specific manner. To this end, this paper proposes an active style-content dual-branch domain adaptation method specifically designed for semi-supervised object detection in SAR images. The proposed approach employs Task-aware Active Sampling (TAS) module to select the most valuable SAR samples, addressing inefficiencies in random sampling. Also, we employ a dual-branch framework to address the style and content gaps between optical and SAR images. Multi-layer Feature Alignment (MFA) module ensures style alignment by maintaining consistent feature representations across different visual styles, while Gaussian-SAM Image Fusion (G-SIF) module is employed to integrate content from the source domain into the target domain, effectively bridging the gap between optical and SAR images. Extensive experiments on multiple ship and aircraft datasets demonstrate the exceptional generalization capabilities of our proposed model. Published: 2026-03-27T19:53:28+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.855 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xi Yang; Quantao Xie; Yirong Yang; Nannan Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3675888

Relevance: 0.855 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images offer unique advantages in all-weather, all-day remote sensing, but the high acquisition costs and time-consuming annotation processes limit their widespread implementation. Semi-supervised domain adaptation leverages abundant annotated optical images and a small number of labeled SAR images to achieve great performance on SAR images. However, existing semi-supervised domain adaptation object detection methods typically select SAR domain labeled samples randomly, making it difficult to fully exploit the valuable information and distinctive features inherent in the target domain data. Moreover, there is a significant style and content gap between optical and SAR images, and previous methods have not adapted to them in a task-specific manner. To this end, this paper proposes an active style-content dual-branch domain adaptation method specifically designed for semi-supervised object detection in SAR images. The proposed approach employs Task-aware Active Sampling (TAS) module to select the most valuable SAR samples, addressing inefficiencies in random sampling. Also, we employ a dual-branch framework to address the style and content gaps between optical and SAR images. Multi-layer Feature Alignment (MFA) module ensures style alignment by maintaining consistent feature representations across different visual styles, while Gaussian-SAM Image Fusion (G-SIF) module is employed to integrate content from the source domain into the target domain, effectively bridging the gap between optical and SAR images. Extensive experiments on multiple ship and aircraft datasets demonstrate the exceptional generalization capabilities of our proposed model.

item.title: BiPA-Net: Bi-directional prototype alignment with fine-grained enhancement for few-shot object detection
item.dc:creator: Pengrui Duan
Xiaolin Gong
Fengyi Liu
Jinliang Zhang
Shidi Meng
item.description: Few-Shot Object Detection (FSOD) aims to detect novel categories using limited annotated samples while preserving base class performance. However, existing methods are often constrained by insufficient use of fine-grained features and by limited support for high-level semantics. To address these issues, we propose a bi-directional prototype alignment network (BiPA-Net), a framework that integrates fine-grained prototype enhancement, bidirectional adaptive fusion, and a contrastive loss function. Specifically, we introduce a Proto-Enhanced Feature Module (PEFM) that leverages refined dimensional alignment, attentive semantic guidance, and gated weight filtering to facilitate targeted interactions between class prototypes and query features. By employing residual fusion strategies, PEFM enhances numerical stability and preserves key spatial information. Furthermore, to improve high-level semantic aggregation, we design a Bidirectional Adaptive Fusion (BAF) module that establishes a bidirectional, query-support class-aware interaction, allowing features to modulate and reinforce each other. Additionally, we optimize the detection head with an advanced loss function to refine feature discrimination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BiPA-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance on PASCAL VOC with 53.2% and delivers competitive second-best results on MS COCO. Published: 2026-03-26T08:28:10+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.823 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Pengrui Duan; Xiaolin Gong; Fengyi Liu; Jinliang Zhang; Shidi Meng

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133468

Relevance: 0.823 (must_read)

Few-Shot Object Detection (FSOD) aims to detect novel categories using limited annotated samples while preserving base class performance. However, existing methods are often constrained by insufficient use of fine-grained features and by limited support for high-level semantics. To address these issues, we propose a bi-directional prototype alignment network (BiPA-Net), a framework that integrates fine-grained prototype enhancement, bidirectional adaptive fusion, and a contrastive loss function. Specifically, we introduce a Proto-Enhanced Feature Module (PEFM) that leverages refined dimensional alignment, attentive semantic guidance, and gated weight filtering to facilitate targeted interactions between class prototypes and query features. By employing residual fusion strategies, PEFM enhances numerical stability and preserves key spatial information. Furthermore, to improve high-level semantic aggregation, we design a Bidirectional Adaptive Fusion (BAF) module that establishes a bidirectional, query-support class-aware interaction, allowing features to modulate and reinforce each other. Additionally, we optimize the detection head with an advanced loss function to refine feature discrimination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BiPA-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance on PASCAL VOC with 53.2% and delivers competitive second-best results on MS COCO.

item.title: Cross-Modal Generative Feature Fusion Based on Label-Consistency Promoting VAE for Transfer Learning
item.dc:creator: Zebin Wu
Biqi Wang
Yang Xu
Zhihui Wei
item.description: The perpetual challenge in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification lies in the scarcity of labeled samples. Through training the model with source scene data and corresponding labels, and subsequently applying this learning to generalize across target domain data, the cross-scene HSI classification yields favorable outcomes. A novel approach to tackle this issue involves leveraging text information regarding prior knowledge of land cover classes to guide the learning of image features, thereby enhancing the generalization capability of the model. However, the finiteness of both image and text information poses difficulties in improving the training accuracy of the model. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Cross-Modal Generative Network (CMGnet). This network aims to expand available samples by generating diverse samples of images and text, thus mitigating the challenge of inadequate training samples in cross-scene scenarios with limited data. The method employs two branches, one for images and the other for text, to conduct sample diversity generation. To ensure the accuracy of the generated samples, the label consistency constraints are incorporated. The image encoder is trained by the generated images alongside the source domain images, and the generated text features together with the original text features form a cross-domain shared space. In this space, text-image alignment is achieved through supervised contrast learning, thereby facilitating the learning of domain-invariant features guided by linguistic modal. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate the superiority of CMGnet in domain generalization (DG). The codes will be available from the website: https://github.com/wbq330/CMGnet . Published: 2026-03-26T08:38:28+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.819 (must_read)

Journal: Information Fusion

The perpetual challenge in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification lies in the scarcity of labeled samples. Through training the model with source scene data and corresponding labels, and subsequently applying this learning to generalize across target domain data, the cross-scene HSI classification yields favorable outcomes. However, the finiteness of both image and text information poses difficulties in improving the training accuracy of the model. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Cross-Modal Generative Network (CMGnet). This network aims to expand available samples by generating diverse samples of images and text, thus mitigating the challenge of inadequate training samples in cross-scene scenarios with limited data. The method employs two branches, one for images and the other for text, to conduct sample diversity generation. To ensure the accuracy of the generated samples, the label consistency constraints are incorporated. The image encoder is trained by the generated images alongside the source domain images, and the generated text features together with the original text features form a cross-domain shared space. In this space, text-image alignment is achieved through supervised contrast learning, thereby facilitating the learning of domain-invariant features guided by linguistic modal. item.title: Less is More: Infrared and Visible Images Fusion via Semantic-Guided Mixture of Multi-Feature Experts
item.dc:creator: Yinghui Xing
Zhilong Niu
Shuo Yang
Shizhou Zhang
Yanning Zhang
item.description: Infrared (IR) and visible image fusion (IVIF) has become prevalent in recent years. By leveraging the complementary characteristics of infrared and visible images, we can obtain visually-appealing fused images, which further facilitate subsequent scene understanding and object detection from day to night. Integrating complementary information while simultaneously eliminating redundancy is a crucial challenge in fusion. Most of available deep learning based methods, after being trained, execute static inference on all pairs of infrared and visible images. They struggle to effectively handle redundancy of modality across diverse scenarios, resulting in superfluous information such as thermal noise in infrared images and artifacts in visible images. In this paper, we propose an IVIF method based on a semantic-guided mixture of multi-feature experts, where multiple types of features are extracted, each assigned to a dedicated expert network specialized in processing a specific type of features. Through an expert routing mechanism, these experts are chosen dynamically, ensuring that the most significant features of each image modality are routed to a specific group of experts. In order to align fusion task with subsequent semantic segmentation task, we introduce a segmentation head to semantically guide the selection of the complementary features. Extensive experiments on five infrared and visible image fusion and segmentation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, both for image fusion and subsequent semantic segmentation tasks. The code will be available at https://github.com/ZhilongNiu/SD-MoMFE. Published: 2026-03-26T19:52:55+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.811 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yinghui Xing; Zhilong Niu; Shuo Yang; Shizhou Zhang; Yanning Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3675500

Relevance: 0.811 (must_read)

Infrared (IR) and visible image fusion (IVIF) has become prevalent in recent years. By leveraging the complementary characteristics of infrared and visible images, we can obtain visually-appealing fused images, which further facilitate subsequent scene understanding and object detection from day to night. Integrating complementary information while simultaneously eliminating redundancy is a crucial challenge in fusion. Most of available deep learning based methods, after being trained, execute static inference on all pairs of infrared and visible images. They struggle to effectively handle redundancy of modality across diverse scenarios, resulting in superfluous information such as thermal noise in infrared images and artifacts in visible images. In this paper, we propose an IVIF method based on a semantic-guided mixture of multi-feature experts, where multiple types of features are extracted, each assigned to a dedicated expert network specialized in processing a specific type of features. Through an expert routing mechanism, these experts are chosen dynamically, ensuring that the most significant features of each image modality are routed to a specific group of experts. In order to align fusion task with subsequent semantic segmentation task, we introduce a segmentation head to semantically guide the selection of the complementary features. Extensive experiments on five infrared and visible image fusion and segmentation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, both for image fusion and subsequent semantic segmentation tasks. The code will be available at https://github.com/ZhilongNiu/SD-MoMFE.

item.title: Multi-Granularity Token Learning for Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Muxin Liao
Yingqiong Peng
Yuting Sun
Wenju Huang
Yinglong Wang
Kun Zhan
Yuhang Zhang
item.description: Recent Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) methods have harnessed vision foundation models (VFMs) to achieve remarkable performance in unseen domains. Two key issues require immediate attention from these methods. First, these methods ignore the learning of contextual information, since VFMs are always pretrained on the image classification task, which means that the frozen backbone of these VFMs struggles to extract contextual information used for dense prediction tasks. Second, these methods focus on enhancing domain-invariant information within the learnable tokens or the features extracted by the frozen backbone (e.g., normalizing their style variances), inevitably losing the effective information. To address these two key issues, this paper proposes a Multi-Granularity Token Learning (MGTL) approach to fine-tune VFMs for DGSS, which uses the visual and text embeddings from the CLIP model for optimizing a set of learnable tokens to learn the domain-invariant information of two granularities, including the domain-invariant contextual and conceptual information. Specifically, first, a contextual relation learning strategy is proposed to assist tokens in capturing and embedding contextual information into VFMs, which is crucial to the semantic segmentation task. Second, a conceptual learning strategy is proposed to acquire instance-level conceptual information for the set of learnable tokens and embed it into VFMs to enhance instance-level domain-invariant information. The proposed MGTL approach is evaluated on multiple DGSS benchmarks, achieving superior performance to recent DGSS methods while maintaining low computational cost in trainable parameters during the fine-tuning stage. Published: 2026-03-26T19:50:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.811 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Muxin Liao; Yingqiong Peng; Yuting Sun; Wenju Huang; Yinglong Wang; Kun Zhan; Yuhang Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3678041

Relevance: 0.811 (must_read)

Recent Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) methods have harnessed vision foundation models (VFMs) to achieve remarkable performance in unseen domains. Two key issues require immediate attention from these methods. First, these methods ignore the learning of contextual information, since VFMs are always pretrained on the image classification task, which means that the frozen backbone of these VFMs struggles to extract contextual information used for dense prediction tasks. Second, these methods focus on enhancing domain-invariant information within the learnable tokens or the features extracted by the frozen backbone (e.g., normalizing their style variances), inevitably losing the effective information. To address these two key issues, this paper proposes a Multi-Granularity Token Learning (MGTL) approach to fine-tune VFMs for DGSS, which uses the visual and text embeddings from the CLIP model for optimizing a set of learnable tokens to learn the domain-invariant information of two granularities, including the domain-invariant contextual and conceptual information. Specifically, first, a contextual relation learning strategy is proposed to assist tokens in capturing and embedding contextual information into VFMs, which is crucial to the semantic segmentation task. Second, a conceptual learning strategy is proposed to acquire instance-level conceptual information for the set of learnable tokens and embed it into VFMs to enhance instance-level domain-invariant information. The proposed MGTL approach is evaluated on multiple DGSS benchmarks, achieving superior performance to recent DGSS methods while maintaining low computational cost in trainable parameters during the fine-tuning stage.

item.title: Mask-CDKD: A source-free and label-free cross-domain knowledge distillation framework from SAM for satellite onboard VHR land-cover mapping
item.dc:creator: Daoyu Shu
Zhan Zhang
Xiao Huang
Ru Wang
Nan Jia
Xinzhe Fu
Bingnan Yang
Fang Wan
Jianzhong Lu
Jianya Gong
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Real-time onboard land use and land cover (LULC) mapping from very-high-resolution remote sensing (VHR-RS) imagery is critical for time-sensitive applications but is constrained by limited computation, memory, and power on satellite platforms. Lightweight remote sensing (RS) semantic segmentation models for onboard deployment often suffer from limited representational capacity and degraded accuracy under large-scale variation and regional heterogeneity, while large segmentation foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) are prohibitively expensive for direct deployment. Knowledge distillation (KD) offers a path to transfer SAM’s priors into compact models. However, cross-domain mismatch between natural-image teachers and VHR-RS students introduces significant bias. While existing cross-domain knowledge distillation (CDKD) methods can alleviate this mismatch, they either require costly labeled source data or adopt rigid source-free decoupling that fails under complex VHR-RS conditions and leaves residual bias. To address these challenges, we propose Mask-CDKD, a source-free and label-free cross-domain knowledge distillation framework for natural-image-to-VHR-RS distillation that transfers a pretrained SAM teacher’s priors to a lightweight student model. Mask-CDKD exploits masked image modeling as implicit supervision for target-domain representation learning and integrates a multi-scale Mixture-of-Adapters (MMoA) module with adaptive gating to fuse spatial cues and suppress domain-specific interference, while a single-stage bidirectional KD scheme stabilizes teacher–student alignment. To facilitate this paradigm, we curate LuoJiaCDKD-100K, a dataset of 100,801 standardized o = " role="presentation"> = = 1024 × 1024,-pixel VHR-RS images with global coverage. Extensive experiments on DeepGlobe, c = " role="presentation"> = = Wuhan,cp = " role="presentation"> = = , p = " role="presentation"> = = 1, and GF-series demonstrate that Mask-CDKD consistently outperforms fully supervised networks (+6.72%), lightweight SAM variants (+5.21%), parameter-efficient SAM adaptations (+2.62%), and RS foundation encoders (+1.51%) in terms of group-wise average mIoU, with additional gains achieved by the LuoJiaNET implementation. In addition, the distilled student model is deployed on NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX with competitive accuracy under realistic memory and power constraints. These results demonstrate that the lightweight models obtained via Mask-CDKD effectively balance accuracy and efficiency, indicating strong potential for practical onboard VHR-RS LULC mapping. Code and resources are available at https://github.com/whujader/mask_cdkd . Published: 2026-03-26T08:42:55+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Daoyu Shu; Zhan Zhang; Xiao Huang; Ru Wang; Nan Jia; Xinzhe Fu; Bingnan Yang; Fang Wan; Jianzhong Lu; Jianya Gong

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.035

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Real-time onboard land use and land cover (LULC) mapping from very-high-resolution remote sensing (VHR-RS) imagery is critical for time-sensitive applications but is constrained by limited computation, memory, and power on satellite platforms. Lightweight remote sensing (RS) semantic segmentation models for onboard deployment often suffer from limited representational capacity and degraded accuracy under large-scale variation and regional heterogeneity, while large segmentation foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) are prohibitively expensive for direct deployment. Knowledge distillation (KD) offers a path to transfer SAM’s priors into compact models. However, cross-domain mismatch between natural-image teachers and VHR-RS students introduces significant bias. While existing cross-domain knowledge distillation (CDKD) methods can alleviate this mismatch, they either require costly labeled source data or adopt rigid source-free decoupling that fails under complex VHR-RS conditions and leaves residual bias. To address these challenges, we propose Mask-CDKD, a source-free and label-free cross-domain knowledge distillation framework for natural-image-to-VHR-RS distillation that transfers a pretrained SAM teacher’s priors to a lightweight student model. Mask-CDKD exploits masked image modeling as implicit supervision for target-domain representation learning and integrates a multi-scale Mixture-of-Adapters (MMoA) module with adaptive gating to fuse spatial cues and suppress domain-specific interference, while a single-stage bidirectional KD scheme stabilizes teacher–student alignment. To facilitate this paradigm, we curate LuoJiaCDKD-100K, a dataset of 100,801 standardized o = " role="presentation"> = = 1024 × 1024,-pixel VHR-RS images with global coverage. Extensive experiments on DeepGlobe, c = " role="presentation"> = = Wuhan,cp = " role="presentation"> = = , p = " role="presentation"> = = 1, and GF-series demonstrate that Mask-CDKD consistently outperforms fully supervised networks (+6.72%), lightweight SAM variants (+5.21%), parameter-efficient SAM adaptations (+2.62%), and RS foundation encoders (+1.51%) in terms of group-wise average mIoU, with additional gains achieved by the LuoJiaNET implementation. In addition, the distilled student model is deployed on NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX with competitive accuracy under realistic memory and power constraints. These results demonstrate that the lightweight models obtained via Mask-CDKD effectively balance accuracy and efficiency, indicating strong potential for practical onboard VHR-RS LULC mapping. Code and resources are available at https://github.com/whujader/mask_cdkd .

item.title: GoldiCLIP: The Goldilocks Approach for Balancing Explicit Supervision for Language-Image Pretraining
item.dc:creator: Deen Dayal Mohan
Hossein Souri
Vitali Petsiuk
Juhong Min
Gopal Sharma
Luowei Zhou
Suren Kumar
item.description: Until recently, the success of large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has primarily relied on billion-sample datasets, posing a significant barrier to progress. Latest works have begun to close this gap by improving supervision quality, but each addresses only a subset of the weaknesses in contrastive pretraining. We present GoldiCLIP, a framework built on a Goldilocks principle of finding the right balance of supervision signals. Our multifaceted training framework synergistically combines three key innovations: (1) a text-conditioned self-distillation method to align both text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) an encoder integrated decoder with Visual Question Answering (VQA) objective that enables the encoder to generalize beyond the caption-like queries; and (3) an uncertainty-based weighting mechanism that automatically balances all heterogeneous losses. Trained on just 30 million images, 300x less data than leading methods, GoldiCLIP achieves state-of-the-art among data-efficient approaches, improving over the best comparable baseline by 2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, 2.0 on fine-grained retrieval, and 5.9 on question-based retrieval, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models. Project page: https://petsi.uk/goldiclip. Published: 2026-03-25T20:33:25+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Deen Dayal Mohan; Hossein Souri; Vitali Petsiuk; Juhong Min; Gopal Sharma; Luowei Zhou; Suren Kumar

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Until recently, the success of large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has primarily relied on billion-sample datasets, posing a significant barrier to progress. Latest works have begun to close this gap by improving supervision quality, but each addresses only a subset of the weaknesses in contrastive pretraining. We present GoldiCLIP, a framework built on a Goldilocks principle of finding the right balance of supervision signals. Our multifaceted training framework synergistically combines three key innovations: (1) a text-conditioned self-distillation method to align both text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) an encoder integrated decoder with Visual Question Answering (VQA) objective that enables the encoder to generalize beyond the caption-like queries; and (3) an uncertainty-based weighting mechanism that automatically balances all heterogeneous losses. Trained on just 30 million images, 300x less data than leading methods, GoldiCLIP achieves state-of-the-art among data-efficient approaches, improving over the best comparable baseline by 2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, 2.0 on fine-grained retrieval, and 5.9 on question-based retrieval, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models. Project page: https://petsi.uk/goldiclip.

item.title: Learning in Diversity: Empowering Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation with Style Injection
item.dc:creator: Runtong Zhang
Fanman Meng
Haoran Wei
Qingbo Wu
Linfeng Xu
Hongliang Li
item.description: Data augmentation is widely employed in Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) to address the challenge of training models that generalize to unseen target domains. However, existing methods, such as applying random transformations or textual prompts, often generate limited diversity due to noise corruption and insufficient fine-grained guidance. To overcome these limitations, we propose SID, a novel Style- Injection-based DGSS method, which leverages style images to enhance data diversity and provide fine-grained visual guidance for representing target domains. Our approach introduces three key components: (1) a Content-Style Fusion (CSF) module that extracts and injects style features into content features using multi-headed self-attention and a style injection loss, effectively aligning feature distributions with target domains; (2) an Attention-based Semantic Preservation (ASP) layer embedded in CSF to capture semantic correlations among patches and support robust segmentation; and (3) a Style-Conditioned Low- Rank Adaptation (SC-LoRA) module that constructs a unified style space using style prototypes and embeds stylized features for effective alignment. Unlike conventional two-stage methods relying on image-level augmentation, SID adopts an end-toend framework with feature-level style injection, significantly reducing computational overhead. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate that SID introduces a regularization term to the DGSS objective and explicitly improves the robustness of the segmentation head. Extensive cross-domain experiments demonstrate that SID achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code will be publicly released. Published: 2026-03-26T19:50:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Runtong Zhang; Fanman Meng; Haoran Wei; Qingbo Wu; Linfeng Xu; Hongliang Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3678074

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Data augmentation is widely employed in Domain Generalized Semantic Segmentation (DGSS) to address the challenge of training models that generalize to unseen target domains. However, existing methods, such as applying random transformations or textual prompts, often generate limited diversity due to noise corruption and insufficient fine-grained guidance. To overcome these limitations, we propose SID, a novel Style- Injection-based DGSS method, which leverages style images to enhance data diversity and provide fine-grained visual guidance for representing target domains. Our approach introduces three key components: (1) a Content-Style Fusion (CSF) module that extracts and injects style features into content features using multi-headed self-attention and a style injection loss, effectively aligning feature distributions with target domains; (2) an Attention-based Semantic Preservation (ASP) layer embedded in CSF to capture semantic correlations among patches and support robust segmentation; and (3) a Style-Conditioned Low- Rank Adaptation (SC-LoRA) module that constructs a unified style space using style prototypes and embeds stylized features for effective alignment. Unlike conventional two-stage methods relying on image-level augmentation, SID adopts an end-toend framework with feature-level style injection, significantly reducing computational overhead. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate that SID introduces a regularization term to the DGSS objective and explicitly improves the robustness of the segmentation head. Extensive cross-domain experiments demonstrate that SID achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code will be publicly released.

item.title: Cross-Modal Prototype Alignment and Mixing for Training-Free Few-Shot Classification
item.dc:creator: Dipam Goswami
Simone Magistri
Gido M. van de Ven
Bartłomiej Twardowski
Andrew D. Bagdanov
Tinne Tuytelaars
Joost van de Weijer
item.description: Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP are trained with the objective of aligning text and image pairs. To improve CLIP-based few-shot image classification, recent works have observed that, along with text embeddings, image embeddings from the training set are an important source of information. In this work we investigate the impact of directly mixing image and text prototypes for few-shot classification and analyze this from a bias-variance perspective. We show that mixing prototypes acts like a shrinkage estimator. Although mixed prototypes improve classification performance, the image prototypes still add some noise in the form of instance-specific background or context information. In order to capture only information from the image space relevant to the given classification task, we propose projecting image prototypes onto the principal directions of the semantic text embedding space to obtain a text-aligned semantic image subspace. These text-aligned image prototypes, when mixed with text embeddings, further improve classification. However, for downstream datasets with poor cross-modal alignment in CLIP, semantic alignment might be suboptimal. We show that the image subspace can still be leveraged by modeling the anisotropy using class covariances. We demonstrate that combining a text-aligned mixed prototype classifier and an image-specific LDA classifier outperforms existing methods across few-shot classification benchmarks. Published: 2026-03-25T17:04:43+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dipam Goswami; Simone Magistri; Gido M. van de Ven; Bartłomiej Twardowski; Andrew D. Bagdanov; Tinne Tuytelaars; Joost van de Weijer

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP are trained with the objective of aligning text and image pairs. To improve CLIP-based few-shot image classification, recent works have observed that, along with text embeddings, image embeddings from the training set are an important source of information. In this work we investigate the impact of directly mixing image and text prototypes for few-shot classification and analyze this from a bias-variance perspective. We show that mixing prototypes acts like a shrinkage estimator. Although mixed prototypes improve classification performance, the image prototypes still add some noise in the form of instance-specific background or context information. In order to capture only information from the image space relevant to the given classification task, we propose projecting image prototypes onto the principal directions of the semantic text embedding space to obtain a text-aligned semantic image subspace. These text-aligned image prototypes, when mixed with text embeddings, further improve classification. However, for downstream datasets with poor cross-modal alignment in CLIP, semantic alignment might be suboptimal. We show that the image subspace can still be leveraged by modeling the anisotropy using class covariances. We demonstrate that combining a text-aligned mixed prototype classifier and an image-specific LDA classifier outperforms existing methods across few-shot classification benchmarks.

item.title: VISion On Request: Enhanced VLLM efficiency with sparse, dynamically selected, vision-language interactions
item.dc:creator: Adrian Bulat
Alberto Baldrati
Ioannis Maniadis Metaxas
Yassine Ouali
Georgios Tzimiropoulos
item.description: Existing approaches for improving the efficiency of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are largely based on the concept of visual token reduction. This approach, however, creates an information bottleneck that impairs performance, especially on challenging tasks that require fine-grained understanding and reasoning. In this work, we challenge this paradigm by introducing VISion On Request (VISOR), a method that reduces inference cost without discarding visual information. Instead of compressing the image, VISOR improves efficiency by sparsifying the interaction between image and text tokens. Specifically, the language model attends to the full set of high-resolution visual tokens through a small, strategically placed set of attention layers: general visual context is provided by efficient cross-attention between text-image, while a few well-placed and dynamically selected self-attention layers refine the visual representations themselves, enabling complex, high-resolution reasoning when needed. Based on this principle, we first train a single universal network on a range of computational budgets by varying the number of self-attention layers, and then introduce a lightweight policy mechanism that dynamically allocates visual computation based on per-sample complexity. Extensive experiments show that VISOR drastically reduces computational cost while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks, and excels in challenging tasks that require detailed visual understanding. Published: 2026-03-24T17:58:17+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Adrian Bulat; Alberto Baldrati; Ioannis Maniadis Metaxas; Yassine Ouali; Georgios Tzimiropoulos

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Existing approaches for improving the efficiency of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are largely based on the concept of visual token reduction. This approach, however, creates an information bottleneck that impairs performance, especially on challenging tasks that require fine-grained understanding and reasoning. In this work, we challenge this paradigm by introducing VISion On Request (VISOR), a method that reduces inference cost without discarding visual information. Instead of compressing the image, VISOR improves efficiency by sparsifying the interaction between image and text tokens. Specifically, the language model attends to the full set of high-resolution visual tokens through a small, strategically placed set of attention layers: general visual context is provided by efficient cross-attention between text-image, while a few well-placed and dynamically selected self-attention layers refine the visual representations themselves, enabling complex, high-resolution reasoning when needed. Based on this principle, we first train a single universal network on a range of computational budgets by varying the number of self-attention layers, and then introduce a lightweight policy mechanism that dynamically allocates visual computation based on per-sample complexity. Extensive experiments show that VISOR drastically reduces computational cost while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks, and excels in challenging tasks that require detailed visual understanding.

item.title: OptiSAR-Net++: A Large-Scale Benchmark and Transformer-Free Framework for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
item.dc:creator: Xiaoyu Tang
Jun Dong
Jintao Cheng
Rui Fan
item.description: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize specific targets in remote sensing images using natural language expressions. However, existing methods are restricted to single-sensor domains, i.e., either optical or synthetic aperture radar (SAR), limiting their real-world applicability. In this paper, we introduce the Cross-Domain RSVG (CD-RSVG) task and construct OptSAR-RSVG, the first large-scale benchmark dataset for this setting. To tackle the challenges of cross-domain feature modeling, computational inefficiency, and fine-grained semantic discrimination, we propose OptiSAR-Net++. Our framework features a patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for efficient cross-domain feature decoupling. To mitigate the substantial computational overhead of Transformer decoding frameworks, we adopt a CLIP-based contrastive paradigm and further incorporate dynamic adversarial negative sampling, thereby transforming generative regression into an efficient cross-modal matching process. Additionally, a text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA) and a region-aware auxiliary head are introduced to enhance semantic-visual alignment and spatial modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OptiSAR-Net++ achieves SOTA performance on both OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks, offering significant advantages in localization accuracy and efficiency. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available. Published: 2026-03-25T23:46:57+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaoyu Tang; Jun Dong; Jintao Cheng; Rui Fan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize specific targets in remote sensing images using natural language expressions. However, existing methods are restricted to single-sensor domains, i.e., either optical or synthetic aperture radar (SAR), limiting their real-world applicability. In this paper, we introduce the Cross-Domain RSVG (CD-RSVG) task and construct OptSAR-RSVG, the first large-scale benchmark dataset for this setting. To tackle the challenges of cross-domain feature modeling, computational inefficiency, and fine-grained semantic discrimination, we propose OptiSAR-Net++. Our framework features a patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for efficient cross-domain feature decoupling. To mitigate the substantial computational overhead of Transformer decoding frameworks, we adopt a CLIP-based contrastive paradigm and further incorporate dynamic adversarial negative sampling, thereby transforming generative regression into an efficient cross-modal matching process. Additionally, a text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA) and a region-aware auxiliary head are introduced to enhance semantic-visual alignment and spatial modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OptiSAR-Net++ achieves SOTA performance on both OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks, offering significant advantages in localization accuracy and efficiency. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available.

item.title: HDC-DETR: Hierarchical Detail and Context Enhancement DETR for Tiny Object Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
item.dc:creator: Tingting Qiao
He Chen
Guoqing Wang
Wenchao Liu
Jue Wang
Yizhuang Xie
Xiaodong Gong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Accurately detecting tiny objects in remote sensing imagery remains challenging, primarily due to weak detail representation and complex background interference, which stem from inadequate modeling of fine-grained features and limited ability to distinguish targets from visually similar contexts. Existing advanced DETR-based detectors often struggle with these issues owing to insufficient sensitivity to subtle details and limited capability for contextual modeling. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Hierarchical Detail and Context Enhancement Detection Transformer (HDC-DETR), a hierarchical query-based detection framework specifically designed for tiny object detection in remote sensing imagery. HDC-DETR explicitly enhances frequency-domain detail perception and spatial contextual modeling through two dedicated modules. The Frequency-Statistics Adaptive Attention Module (FSAAM) employs discrete wavelet transform to decompose multi-scale features and introduces a Local–Global Probability-Density Attention (LG-PDA) to selectively enhance statistically distinctive high-frequency edges and textures, thereby improving fine-grained detail discriminability. Meanwhile, the Hierarchical Context Modulation Block (HCMB) adaptively aggregates multi-scale spatial contexts and employs spatial gating mechanisms to selectively enhance foreground features and suppress ambiguous backgrounds, significantly improving foreground–background discriminability. Comprehensive evaluations conducted on the AI-TOD and LEVIR-Ship benchmarks demonstrate that HDC-DETR achieves superior performance in detecting tiny objects, especially under complex remote sensing scenarios. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in simultaneously enhancing fine-grained detail perception and spatial contextual modeling. Published: 2026-03-26T19:50:18+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.805 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tingting Qiao; He Chen; Guoqing Wang; Wenchao Liu; Jue Wang; Yizhuang Xie; Xiaodong Gong

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3677872

Relevance: 0.805 (must_read)

Accurately detecting tiny objects in remote sensing imagery remains challenging, primarily due to weak detail representation and complex background interference, which stem from inadequate modeling of fine-grained features and limited ability to distinguish targets from visually similar contexts. Existing advanced DETR-based detectors often struggle with these issues owing to insufficient sensitivity to subtle details and limited capability for contextual modeling. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Hierarchical Detail and Context Enhancement Detection Transformer (HDC-DETR), a hierarchical query-based detection framework specifically designed for tiny object detection in remote sensing imagery. HDC-DETR explicitly enhances frequency-domain detail perception and spatial contextual modeling through two dedicated modules. The Frequency-Statistics Adaptive Attention Module (FSAAM) employs discrete wavelet transform to decompose multi-scale features and introduces a Local–Global Probability-Density Attention (LG-PDA) to selectively enhance statistically distinctive high-frequency edges and textures, thereby improving fine-grained detail discriminability. Meanwhile, the Hierarchical Context Modulation Block (HCMB) adaptively aggregates multi-scale spatial contexts and employs spatial gating mechanisms to selectively enhance foreground features and suppress ambiguous backgrounds, significantly improving foreground–background discriminability. Comprehensive evaluations conducted on the AI-TOD and LEVIR-Ship benchmarks demonstrate that HDC-DETR achieves superior performance in detecting tiny objects, especially under complex remote sensing scenarios. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in simultaneously enhancing fine-grained detail perception and spatial contextual modeling.

item.title: EarthMapper: Visual Autoregressive Models for Controllable Bidirectional Satellite–Map Translation
item.dc:creator: Yuzhe Sun
Zhe Dong
Haochen Jiang
Tianzhu Liu
Yanfeng Gu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Cross-modal translation between satellite imagery and cartographic maps constitutes a foundational challenge in geospatial artificial intelligence, with critical applications ranging from autonomous mapping systems to disaster response infrastructures. While existing methods attempt to address unidirectional satellite-to-map translation, the bidirectional satellite-map translation (BSMT) problem remains formally undefined and technically under-explored, particularly due to: (i) non-differentiable geometric discrepancies between pixel-level observations and symbolic representations, causing cross-modal feature misalignment; (ii) conflicting learning objectives requiring simultaneous high-level geographic abstraction and pixel-wise photorealism. To address these limitations, we introduce EarthMapper, a novel autoregressive framework for controllable bidirectional satellite-map translation. EarthMapper employs geographic coordinate embeddings to anchor generation, ensuring region-specific adaptability, and leverages multi-scale feature alignment within a geo-conditioned joint scale autoregression (GJSA) process to unify bidirectional translation in a single training cycle. A semantic infusion (SI) mechanism is introduced to enhance feature-level consistency, while a key point adaptive guidance (KPAG) mechanism is proposed to dynamically balance diversity and precision during inference. We further contribute CNSatMap, a large-scale dataset comprising 302,132 precisely aligned satellite-map pairs across 38 Chinese cities, enabling robust benchmarking. Extensive experiments on CNSatMap and the New York dataset demonstrate EarthMapper’s superior performance, achieving significant improvements in visual realism, semantic consistency, and structural fidelity over state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, EarthMapper excels in zero-shot tasks like in-painting, out-painting, and coordinate-conditional generation, underscoring its versatility. The source code for EarthMapper and ... Published: 2026-03-27T19:49:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.804 (must_read)

Authors: Yuzhe Sun; Zhe Dong; Haochen Jiang; Tianzhu Liu; Yanfeng Gu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3678241

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Cross-modal translation between satellite imagery and cartographic maps constitutes a foundational challenge in geospatial artificial intelligence, with critical applications ranging from autonomous mapping systems to disaster response infrastructures. While existing methods attempt to address unidirectional satellite-to-map translation, the bidirectional satellite-map translation (BSMT) problem remains formally undefined and technically under-explored, particularly due to: (i) non-differentiable geometric discrepancies between pixel-level observations and symbolic representations, causing cross-modal feature misalignment; (ii) conflicting learning objectives requiring simultaneous high-level geographic abstraction and pixel-wise photorealism. To address these limitations, we introduce EarthMapper, a novel autoregressive framework for controllable bidirectional satellite-map translation. EarthMapper employs geographic coordinate embeddings to anchor generation, ensuring region-specific adaptability, and leverages multi-scale feature alignment within a geo-conditioned joint scale autoregression (GJSA) process to unify bidirectional translation in a single training cycle. A semantic infusion (SI) mechanism is introduced to enhance feature-level consistency, while a key point adaptive guidance (KPAG) mechanism is proposed to dynamically balance diversity and precision during inference. We further contribute CNSatMap, a large-scale dataset comprising 302,132 precisely aligned satellite-map pairs across 38 Chinese cities, enabling robust benchmarking. Extensive experiments on CNSatMap and the New York dataset demonstrate EarthMapper’s superior performance, achieving significant improvements in visual realism, semantic consistency, and structural fidelity over state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, EarthMapper excels in zero-shot tasks like in-painting, out-painting, and coordinate-conditional generation, underscoring its versatility. The source code for EarthMapper and ...

item.title: Unlocking Few-Shot Capabilities in LVLMs via Prompt Conditioning and Head Selection
item.dc:creator: Adhemar de Senneville
Xavier Bou
Jérémy Anger
Rafael Grompone
Gabriele Facciolo
item.description: Current Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) excel at many zero-shot tasks like image captioning, visual question answering and OCR. However, these same models suffer from poor performance at image classification tasks, underperforming against CLIP-based methods. Notably, this gap is surprising because many LVLMs use CLIP-pretrained vision encoders. Yet LVLMs are not inherently limited by CLIP's architecture with independent vision and text encoders. In CLIP, this separation biases classification toward class-name matching rather than joint visual-text reasoning. In this paper we show that, despite their poor raw performance, LVLMs can improve visual feature class separability at inference using prompt conditioning, and LVLMs' internal representations, especially attention heads, can outperform the model itself at zero-shot and few-shot classification. We introduce Head Ensemble Classifiers (HEC) to bridge the performance gap between CLIP-based and LVLM-based classification methods. Inspired by Gaussian Discriminant Analysis, HEC ranks the most discriminative vision and text heads and combines them into a training-free classifier. We show that HEC achieves state-of-the-art performance in few-shot and zero-shot classification across 12 datasets. Published: 2026-03-25T11:00:22+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Adhemar de Senneville; Xavier Bou; Jérémy Anger; Rafael Grompone; Gabriele Facciolo

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Current Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) excel at many zero-shot tasks like image captioning, visual question answering and OCR. However, these same models suffer from poor performance at image classification tasks, underperforming against CLIP-based methods. Notably, this gap is surprising because many LVLMs use CLIP-pretrained vision encoders. Yet LVLMs are not inherently limited by CLIP's architecture with independent vision and text encoders. In CLIP, this separation biases classification toward class-name matching rather than joint visual-text reasoning. In this paper we show that, despite their poor raw performance, LVLMs can improve visual feature class separability at inference using prompt conditioning, and LVLMs' internal representations, especially attention heads, can outperform the model itself at zero-shot and few-shot classification. We introduce Head Ensemble Classifiers (HEC) to bridge the performance gap between CLIP-based and LVLM-based classification methods. Inspired by Gaussian Discriminant Analysis, HEC ranks the most discriminative vision and text heads and combines them into a training-free classifier. We show that HEC achieves state-of-the-art performance in few-shot and zero-shot classification across 12 datasets.

item.title: Attention-aware Inference Optimizations for Large Vision-Language Models with Memory-efficient Decoding
item.dc:creator: Fatih Ilhan
Gaowen Liu
Ramana Rao Kompella
Selim Furkan Tekin
Tiansheng Huang
Zachary Yahn
Yichang Xu
Ling Liu
item.description: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in multi-modal reasoning, but their inference time efficiency remains a significant challenge due to the memory overhead during decoding, especially when the query and answer of VLMs consist of long sequences of visual and text tokens. This paper presents AttentionPack, an adaptive and attention-aware optimization framework tailored for large vision-language models with improving memory-efficiency during decoding, focusing on addressing the challenges due to the increased high number of visual inputs and interactions, particularly in long-context tasks with multiple high-resolution images or videos. AttentionPack is novel in two aspects: (i) We introduce a multi-head attention compaction method for economically storing key and value matrices by exploiting the implicit low-rank structure, and (ii) we develop a token-specific attention-aware decompression mechanism to reduce latency overhead. Experimental results on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AttentionPack improves memory efficiency by up to 8x, enabling higher batch sizes and faster batch inference while preserving the model output quality or longer context lengths for superior retrieval performance. We also report the effectiveness of AttentionPack combined with eviction, quantization and kernel fusion, showing further efficiency gains for resource-limited environments. Published: 2026-03-25T04:01:10+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Fatih Ilhan; Gaowen Liu; Ramana Rao Kompella; Selim Furkan Tekin; Tiansheng Huang; Zachary Yahn; Yichang Xu; Ling Liu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in multi-modal reasoning, but their inference time efficiency remains a significant challenge due to the memory overhead during decoding, especially when the query and answer of VLMs consist of long sequences of visual and text tokens. This paper presents AttentionPack, an adaptive and attention-aware optimization framework tailored for large vision-language models with improving memory-efficiency during decoding, focusing on addressing the challenges due to the increased high number of visual inputs and interactions, particularly in long-context tasks with multiple high-resolution images or videos. AttentionPack is novel in two aspects: (i) We introduce a multi-head attention compaction method for economically storing key and value matrices by exploiting the implicit low-rank structure, and (ii) we develop a token-specific attention-aware decompression mechanism to reduce latency overhead. Experimental results on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AttentionPack improves memory efficiency by up to 8x, enabling higher batch sizes and faster batch inference while preserving the model output quality or longer context lengths for superior retrieval performance. We also report the effectiveness of AttentionPack combined with eviction, quantization and kernel fusion, showing further efficiency gains for resource-limited environments.

item.title: From Species-Specific Models to Universal Re-ID: A Survey of Animal Re-Identification
item.dc:creator: Cigdem Beyan
Anil Osman Tur
Ehsan Karimi
item.description: Automated animal re-identification (Re-ID) has become an essential tool for wildlife ecology, conservation management, and precision livestock farming. Recent progress in deep representation learning, transformer architectures, multimodal learning, and vision–language modeling has accelerated the development of scalable, non-invasive systems for identifying individuals across images and videos. This survey provides a comprehensive review of animal Re-ID research published between 2020 and 2025, encompassing 41 peer-reviewed works. We propose a structured taxonomy of animal Re-ID methods and provide an integrated analysis of approaches, datasets, and evaluation practices. We also highlight persistent challenges, including domain shift, temporal variability, annotation scarcity, and inconsistent evaluation protocols, and outline broad future research directions toward universal, temporally robust, and ecologically meaningful animal Re-ID systems. This survey provides a unified foundation for advancing robust and deployable solutions in the coming decade. Published: 2026-03-26T17:22:55+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cigdem Beyan; Anil Osman Tur; Ehsan Karimi

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104323

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Automated animal re-identification (Re-ID) has become an essential tool for wildlife ecology, conservation management, and precision livestock farming. Recent progress in deep representation learning, transformer architectures, multimodal learning, and vision–language modeling has accelerated the development of scalable, non-invasive systems for identifying individuals across images and videos. This survey provides a comprehensive review of animal Re-ID research published between 2020 and 2025, encompassing 41 peer-reviewed works. We propose a structured taxonomy of animal Re-ID methods and provide an integrated analysis of approaches, datasets, and evaluation practices. We also highlight persistent challenges, including domain shift, temporal variability, annotation scarcity, and inconsistent evaluation protocols, and outline broad future research directions toward universal, temporally robust, and ecologically meaningful animal Re-ID systems. This survey provides a unified foundation for advancing robust and deployable solutions in the coming decade.

item.title: From Insufficient to Sufficient: Hierarchical Semantic Alignment for Remote Sensing Image-Text Retrieval
item.dc:creator: Yuan Shi
Wentao Ma
Shaofan Chen
Lu Liu
Guolong Shi
Zhongyang Yao
Wei Liu
Lichuan Gu
item.description: The objective of Remote Sensing Image-Text Retrieval (RSITR) is to establish a reciprocal semantic correlation between Remote Sensing (RS) visuals and their textual annotations. However, most existing approaches suffer from insufficient cross-modal alignment, as they either perform global-level matching that overlooks local semantics or focus on fine-grained alignment that ignores contextual dependencies. This limitation leads to incomplete and inconsistent semantic correspondence between modalities. Toward this objective, we propose an innovative framework termed Sufficient Hierarchical Semantic Alignment (SHSA), which achieves comprehensive cross-modal understanding via progressive integration of global and local semantics. Specifically, SHSA consists of three key modules: (1) a Textual Semantic Enhancer (TSE) that refines sentence embeddings via a masked language modeling objective to enrich contextual completeness; (2) a Global Cross-modal Matching (GCM) module, which performs a distinguishing global alignment within a joint embedding space using a contrastive learning mechanism; and (3) a Selective Fine-grained Alignment (SFA) module that adaptively identifies informative visual-textual tokens for fine-grained correspondence, optimized with a Token-wise Semantic (TWS) loss. Comprehensive evaluations executed across two widely-used standard datasets, RSICD and RSITMD, indicate that SHSA consistently outperforms State-of-The-Art (SoTA) approaches across multiple metrics, validating its superiority in retrieval accuracy, robustness, and semantic interpretability for complex RS scenarios. Our source codes are available at: https://github.com/hocker-sy/SHSA . Published: 2026-03-26T16:39:52+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuan Shi; Wentao Ma; Shaofan Chen; Lu Liu; Guolong Shi; Zhongyang Yao; Wei Liu; Lichuan Gu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132140

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

The objective of Remote Sensing Image-Text Retrieval (RSITR) is to establish a reciprocal semantic correlation between Remote Sensing (RS) visuals and their textual annotations. However, most existing approaches suffer from insufficient cross-modal alignment, as they either perform global-level matching that overlooks local semantics or focus on fine-grained alignment that ignores contextual dependencies. This limitation leads to incomplete and inconsistent semantic correspondence between modalities. Toward this objective, we propose an innovative framework termed Sufficient Hierarchical Semantic Alignment (SHSA), which achieves comprehensive cross-modal understanding via progressive integration of global and local semantics. Specifically, SHSA consists of three key modules: (1) a Textual Semantic Enhancer (TSE) that refines sentence embeddings via a masked language modeling objective to enrich contextual completeness; (2) a Global Cross-modal Matching (GCM) module, which performs a distinguishing global alignment within a joint embedding space using a contrastive learning mechanism; and (3) a Selective Fine-grained Alignment (SFA) module that adaptively identifies informative visual-textual tokens for fine-grained correspondence, optimized with a Token-wise Semantic (TWS) loss. Comprehensive evaluations executed across two widely-used standard datasets, RSICD and RSITMD, indicate that SHSA consistently outperforms State-of-The-Art (SoTA) approaches across multiple metrics, validating its superiority in retrieval accuracy, robustness, and semantic interpretability for complex RS scenarios. Our source codes are available at: https://github.com/hocker-sy/SHSA .

item.title: ET-SAM: Efficient Point Prompt Prediction in SAM for Unified Scene Text Detection and Layout Analysis
item.dc:creator: Xike Zhang
Maoyuan Ye
Juhua Liu
Bo Du
item.description: Previous works based on Segment Anything Model (SAM) have achieved promising performance in unified scene text detection and layout analysis. However, the typical reliance on pixel-level text segmentation for sampling thousands of foreground points as prompts leads to unsatisfied inference latency and limited data utilization. To address above issues, we propose ET-SAM, an Efficient framework with two decoders for unified scene Text detection and layout analysis based on SAM. Technically, we customize a lightweight point decoder that produces word heatmaps for achieving a few foreground points, thereby eliminating excessive point prompts and accelerating inference. Without the dependence on pixel-level segmentation, we further design a joint training strategy to leverage existing data with heterogeneous text-level annotations. Specifically, the datasets with multi-level, word-level only, and line-level only annotations are combined in parallel as a unified training set. For these datasets, we introduce three corresponding sets of learnable task prompts in both the point decoder and hierarchical mask decoder to mitigate discrepancies across datasets.Extensive experiments demonstrate that, compared to the previous SAM-based architecture, ET-SAM achieves about 3$\times$ inference acceleration while obtaining competitive performance on HierText, and improves an average of 11.0% F-score on Total-Text, CTW1500, and ICDAR15. Published: 2026-03-26T08:37:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xike Zhang; Maoyuan Ye; Juhua Liu; Bo Du

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Previous works based on Segment Anything Model (SAM) have achieved promising performance in unified scene text detection and layout analysis. However, the typical reliance on pixel-level text segmentation for sampling thousands of foreground points as prompts leads to unsatisfied inference latency and limited data utilization. To address above issues, we propose ET-SAM, an Efficient framework with two decoders for unified scene Text detection and layout analysis based on SAM. Technically, we customize a lightweight point decoder that produces word heatmaps for achieving a few foreground points, thereby eliminating excessive point prompts and accelerating inference. Without the dependence on pixel-level segmentation, we further design a joint training strategy to leverage existing data with heterogeneous text-level annotations. Specifically, the datasets with multi-level, word-level only, and line-level only annotations are combined in parallel as a unified training set. For these datasets, we introduce three corresponding sets of learnable task prompts in both the point decoder and hierarchical mask decoder to mitigate discrepancies across datasets.Extensive experiments demonstrate that, compared to the previous SAM-based architecture, ET-SAM achieves about 3$\times$ inference acceleration while obtaining competitive performance on HierText, and improves an average of 11.0% F-score on Total-Text, CTW1500, and ICDAR15.

item.title: Context-Enhanced and Scale-Contrastive Learning for Remote Sensing Imagery Understanding
item.dc:creator: Aiping Hu
Rongting Zhang
Guangyun Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown strong potential for learning representations from unlabeled data, yet its direct application to remote sensing (RS) imagery remains challenging due to complex backgrounds and severe target-scale variations. To address these challenges, we propose Context-Enhanced Semantic Contrast (CESC), a self-supervised pre-training framework tailored for RS imagery. CESC integrates masked reconstruction, explicit contextual enhancement, and cross-scale semantic contrast to jointly model fine-grained spatial structures and scale-invariant semantic representations. Extensive experiments on scene classification and semantic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that CESC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SSL methods under equivalent settings, validating its effectiveness and transferability for RS representation learning. Published: 2026-03-27T19:53:59+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aiping Hu; Rongting Zhang; Guangyun Zhang

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3678388

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown strong potential for learning representations from unlabeled data, yet its direct application to remote sensing (RS) imagery remains challenging due to complex backgrounds and severe target-scale variations. To address these challenges, we propose Context-Enhanced Semantic Contrast (CESC), a self-supervised pre-training framework tailored for RS imagery. CESC integrates masked reconstruction, explicit contextual enhancement, and cross-scale semantic contrast to jointly model fine-grained spatial structures and scale-invariant semantic representations. Extensive experiments on scene classification and semantic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that CESC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SSL methods under equivalent settings, validating its effectiveness and transferability for RS representation learning.

item.title: Class-aware Cross-attention Helps Prompt Learning with Limited Samples
item.dc:creator: Zhimin Zhang
Xiaochen Yang
Xiaoxu Li
Zhanyu Ma
Jing-Hao Xue
item.description: Current prompt learning methods are weak in securing discriminative embeddings with limited samples. To address this issue, we propose class-aware cross-attention prompt learning (CAPrompt). In CAPrompt, on the one hand, image class prototypes estimated from a few labeled samples for each class are integrated into the image encoder. On the other hand, concise class descriptions generated from a large language model are introduced into the text encoder. In both encoders, class-aware information and learnable prompts are integrated via cross-attention, enabling the extraction of highly discriminative image and text embeddings. Our proposed CAPrompt is evaluated on 11 widely used datasets, and the results showcase its effectiveness in achieving the state-of-the-art performance of image classification with limited samples. Our code is available at https://github.com/Zhimin-ZhangCV/CAPrompt. Published: 2026-03-26T19:50:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhimin Zhang; Xiaochen Yang; Xiaoxu Li; Zhanyu Ma; Jing-Hao Xue

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3678027

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Current prompt learning methods are weak in securing discriminative embeddings with limited samples. To address this issue, we propose class-aware cross-attention prompt learning (CAPrompt). In CAPrompt, on the one hand, image class prototypes estimated from a few labeled samples for each class are integrated into the image encoder. On the other hand, concise class descriptions generated from a large language model are introduced into the text encoder. In both encoders, class-aware information and learnable prompts are integrated via cross-attention, enabling the extraction of highly discriminative image and text embeddings. Our proposed CAPrompt is evaluated on 11 widely used datasets, and the results showcase its effectiveness in achieving the state-of-the-art performance of image classification with limited samples. Our code is available at https://github.com/Zhimin-ZhangCV/CAPrompt.

item.title: Recent Advances of Multimodal Continual Learning: A Comprehensive Survey
item.dc:creator: Dianzhi Yu
Xinni Zhang
Yankai Chen
Aiwei Liu
Yifei Zhang
Philip S. Yu
Irwin King
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Continual learning (CL) aims to empower machine learning (ML) models to learn continually from new data, while building upon previously acquired knowledge without forgetting. As models have evolved from small to large pretrained architectures, and from supporting unimodal to multimodal (MM) data, multimodal CL (MMCL) methods have recently emerged. The primary complexity of MMCL is that it extends beyond a simple stacking of unimodal CL methods. Such straightforward approaches often suffer from MM catastrophic forgetting, yielding unsatisfactory performance. In addition, MMCL introduces new challenges that unimodal CL methods fail to adequately address, including modality imbalance, complex modality interaction, high computational costs, and degradation of the pretrained zero-shot capability of MM backbones. In this work, we present the first comprehensive survey on MMCL. We provide essential background knowledge and MMCL settings, as well as a structured taxonomy of MMCL methods. We categorize MMCL methods into four categories, i.e., regularization-based, architecture-based, replay-based, and prompt-based methods, explaining their methodologies and highlighting their key innovations. Additionally, to prompt further research in this field, we summarize open MMCL datasets and benchmarks, provide an in-depth discussion, and discuss several promising future directions. We have also created a GitHub repository for indexing relevant MMCL articles and open resources available at https://github.com/LucyDYu/Awesome-Multimodal-Continual-Learning. Published: 2026-03-26T19:50:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.798 (must_read)
